task: Write a Python function trimmed_mean(nums) returning the mean of the list after dropping exactly one occurrence of the minimum and one occurrence of the maximum, as a float. An empty list returns None. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The solution meets all the criteria, but 'code is concise and readable' could be improved with more comments or docstrings for better clarity.
trace: In this implementation, the function `trimmed_mean()` sorts the input list and checks for edge cases. If the list is empty or contains less than three elements, it returns `None`. Otherwise, it calculates the sum of the middle values after dropping one occurrence of the minimum and one occurrence of the maximum, then divides by the new length to compute the average (trimmed mean). To handle edge cases with lists having an odd number of elements, I chose to exclude the minimum or maximum value from both ends while calculating the sum.
